questions.question: What does Sal learn about herself while watching this family interaction?

responses.content: That Phoebe shouted at her mother. "You don't have to bake things for me. I'm too fat. And you don't have to wait here for me to come home. I'm thirteen now."

That Phoebe marched upstairs. Mrs. Winterbottom offered me a brownie, so I sat down at the table. What I started doing was remembering the day before my mother left. I did not know it was to be her last day home. Several times that day, my mother asked me if I wanted to walk up in the fields with her. It was drizzling outside, and I was cleaning out my desk, and I just did not feel like going. "Maybe later," I kept saying. When she asked me for about the tenth time, I said, "No! I don't want to go. Why do you keep asking me?" I don't know why I did that. I didn't mean anything by it, but that was one of the last memories she had of me, and I wished I could take it back.
That Phoebe marched upstairs. Mrs. Winterbottom offered me a brownie, so I sat down at the table. What I started doing was remembering the day before my mother left.

questions.question:
Rewrite this excerpt of Walk Two Moons with Mrs. Winterbottom as the narrator instead of Sal.


Use evidence explicitly stated in the text, as well as inferences drawn from the text, to identify the narrator's relationship with the other characters.


In your narrative, select language that reflects an appropriate tone for Mrs. Winterbottom.

responses.content: That I walked home, I thought about the message. In the course of a lifetime, what does it matter? I said it over and over. I wondered about the mysterious messenger, and I wondered about all the things in the course of a lifetime that would not matter. I did not think cheerleading tryouts would matter, but I was not so sure about yelling at your mother. I was certain, however, that if your mother left, it would be something that mattered in the whole long course of your lifetime.

questions.question: How does Papa feel about Mr. Avery and Mr. Lanier's decision to stop shopping in Vicksburg?

responses.content: Which paragraph
Which paragraph

That When no one said anything, Mr. Avery glanced at Mr. Lanier, and Mr. Lanier shook his head and continued. "Mr. Granger making it hard on us, David. The way cotton sells these days, seems the more we plant, the less money we gets anyways—"

(27) Mr. Avery's coughing interrupted him and he waited patiently until the coughing had stopped before he went on. "I'm gonna be hard put to pay that debt in Vicksburg, David, but I'm gonna. ...I want you to know that." ...

(28) Mr. Avery's coughing started again and for a while there was only the coughing and the silence. But when the coughing ceased, Mr. Lanier said, "I pray to God there was a way we could stay in this thing, but we can't go on no chain gang, David."

(29) Papa nodded. "Don't expect you to, Silas."

(30) Mr. Avery laughed softly. "We sure had 'em goin' for a time though, didn't we?"

(31) "Yes," agreed Papa quietly, "we sure did."

(32) When the men had left, Stacey snapped, "They got no right pulling out! Just 'cause them Wallaces threaten them one time they go jumping all over themselves to get out like a bunch of scared jackrabbits—"

(33) Papa stood suddenly and grabbed Stacey upward. "You, boy, don't you get so grown you go to talking 'bout more than you know. Them men, they doing what they've gotta do. You got any idea what a risk they took just to go shopping in Vicksburg in the first place? They go on that chain gang and their families got nothing. They'll get kicked off that plot of land they tend and there'll be no place for them to go. You understand that?"

questions.question: Look at paragraphs 46-47. How do Perseus's actions show that he followed Athene's plan?

responses.content: That, Then he wrapped the head in the goat-skin, turning away his eyes, and sprang into the air aloft, faster than he ever sprang before
responses.bucket_rationale: The response directly quotes the text, mentioning that Perseus wrapped the head in the goat-skin, turned away his eyes, and sprang into the air aloft, faster than ever before. This matches the key scene elements required for a 'correct' classification: wrapping the head, turning away, and escaping immediately after striking Medusa. There is no contradiction or fabrication, and the answer is text-specific and accurate.

questions.question: In paragraph 49, Perseus calls the sandals “brave.” What does this reveal about Perseus' character in this moment?

responses.content: It say that, Into the air they sprang yelling, and looked for him who had done the deed. responses.bucket_rationale: The response quotes the text and describes what happens, but does not directly state Perseus's emotional or mental state (such as 'scared' or 'afraid'). The answer shows engagement with the text and references Perseus's reaction, but it does not explicitly identify his feelings. This matches the 'partial-understanding' bucket, as it is text-specific but lacks the explicit naming of the emotional state required for 'correct.'

questions.question:
How do Perseus's responses to individuals and events drive the action of the plot forward? 


Respond to this question in the form of a paragraph that includes a claim, one piece of evidence, and an explanation.

responses.content: Perseus I decisions help move the plot forward. When When Perseus meets the Grey Sisters, he steals their eye to force them to tall him where to go.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response includes a claim (Perseus's decisions help move the plot forward), evidence (he steals the Grey Sisters' eye to force them to tell him where to go), and a brief explanation. However, the explanation only labels the effect (he gets information) rather than clearly connecting how this action drives the plot forward with explicit causal reasoning (such as 'without this, he would not be able to continue his quest'). There is no mechanism phrase or clear cause-effect reasoning, so this is a Score 2 response.

responses.content: Perseus I decisions help move the plot forward. When Perseus meets the Grey Sisters, he steals their eye to force them to tall him where to go. This helps the plot move forward because he gets information obout the Nymphs and Atlas, which leads him closer to Medusa.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response includes a claim (Perseus's decisions help move the plot forward), evidence (he steals the Grey Sisters' eye to force them to tell him where to go), and an explanation that connects the evidence to the claim ('This helps the plot move forward because he gets information about the Nymphs and Atlas, which leads him closer to Medusa'). The phrase 'which leads him closer to Medusa' shows causal reasoning and explains how Perseus's action drives the plot forward. This meets the requirements for score_3.

questions.question: Based on paragraphs 10-12, what can you infer was important to Margaret as a photojournalist?

responses.content: Her mother gave Margaret her first camera in 1921, when she was 17 years old. Her interest in photography grew as a result of her father’s enthusiasm for cameras. A few years later, Margaret’s classmates at Cornell University became her first admirers when photos she took of the campus appeared in the school newspapers. A year after graduating, Margaret moved to Cleveland, Ohio and opened a commercial photography studio.
responses.bucket_rationale:
Step 0: The only highlight to evaluate is: 'Her mother gave Margaret her first camera in 1921, when she was 17 years old. Her interest in photography grew as a result of her father’s enthusiasm for cameras. A few years later, Margaret’s classmates at Cornell University became her first admirers when photos she took of the campus appeared in the school newspapers. A year after graduating, Margaret moved to Cleveland, Ohio and opened a commercial photography studio.'

Step 0A: The longest acceptable answer has 252 characters. My calculated limit = MAX(252 × 1.3, 252 + 50) = MAX(327.6, 302) = 328 characters.

Step 0B: Student's highlight has 410 characters (after normalization).

Step 0C: 410 > 328. The student's highlight exceeds the character limit. Therefore, the answer is incorrect, regardless of content.

responses.content: One of Margaret’s first clients was the Otis Steel Company. Her success was due both to her technique and her skills in dealing with people. At first, several people at the company wondered if a woman could stand up to the intense heat and generally dirty and gritty conditions inside a steel mill. When Margaret finally got permission, the technical problems began. Black-and-white film at that time was sensitive to blue light, not the reds and oranges of hot steel. The pictures came out all black. Margaret solved this problem by bringing along a new style of flare (which produces white light) and having assistants hold them to light her scenes. Her abilities resulted in some of the best steel factory pictures of that era, and these earned her national attention.
responses.bucket_rationale: The student's most recent highlight is much too long and includes several sentences, not just the specific sentence or phrase that describes the problem Margaret encountered or how she solved it. The question asks for a specific sentence or phrase, and the student's response exceeds the acceptable character limit. Therefore, it is marked as incorrect.

questions.question:
How does the author develop the idea that Margaret Bourke-White was a trailblazer? 


Respond to this question in the form of a paragraph that includes your claim, evidence, and explanation.

responses.content: So I think author develops the idea that Margaret Bourke-White was a trailblazer by showing how so. she was a pioneer who went into dangerous places and did jobs usually reserved for men. For example, the text describes how she solved difficult lighting problems at the Otis Steel Company and later became the first female war correspondent during World War II. These details prove she was a trailblazer because she didn't just follow existing rules; she used her courage and technical skills to open up new professional paths that hadn't existed for women before.

The language and reasoning are appropriate for a strong fourth-grade response, and all required elements are present.
